message:
perf(ft-kernel-cpu): pairwise summation in addmm / addmv / dot / bmm

Completes the matmul-family precision arc started in commit 35a7760
(pairwise dot product in matmul). The same naive sequential
accumulator pattern lived in four sibling kernels:

  * `addmm_tensor_contiguous_{f64,f32}` — matmul + bias
  * `addmv_tensor_contiguous_{f64,f32}` — matrix-vector + bias
  * `dot_tensor_contiguous_{f64,f32}` — direct dot product
  * `bmm_tensor_contiguous_{f64,f32}` — batched matmul

For K > 128 (typical d_model in transformers — 768/1024/4096) every
one of these accumulated dot-product error at O(K · ε). PyTorch's
CPU matmul family wraps BLAS which pairwise-sums; this brings
FrankenTorch to the same precision contract.

Implementation: stage the K products into a reusable scratch buffer
allocated once per call (not per output cell), then pairwise-sum the
scratch via the existing `pairwise_sum_{f64,f32}` helper. For
K ≤ 128 the helper falls through to sequential and the result is
bit-identical to the prior implementation (preserves all existing
exact-equality tests).

Tests:
  * `dot_tensor_contiguous_pairwise_precision_at_large_n` — N = 4096
    unit-vector dot product, drift < 1e-14.
  * `bmm_tensor_contiguous_pairwise_precision_at_large_k` —
    3-batch K = 4096 batched matmul, every batch element drift
    < 1e-14. Also confirms the scratch buffer is correctly reused
    across batch iterations.

Quality gates: 12-crate workspace test sweep — ft-kernel-cpu 314 +
ft-api 1091 + ft-conformance 101 + ft-autograd 314 + ft-nn 150 +
ft-optim 74 + ft-data 103 + ft-serialize 15 + ft-runtime 2 +
ft-core 70 + ft-dispatch 9 + ft-device 6 — all green; clippy + ubs
clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+aten::add.Tensor(Tensor self, Tensor other, *, Scalar alpha=1) -> Tensor
+CPU AutogradCPU

@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+  aten::div.Tensor(Tensor self, Tensor other) -> Tensor  
+  CPU | AutogradCPU

@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+aten::min.out(Tensor self, Tensor other, *, Tensor(a!) out) -> Tensor(a!)
+CPU BackendSelect
